content.text: Feature Sunset Communication and Migration Plan

Sunsetting a feature of a Claude-integrated product requires planned communication to every affected user or downstream consumer before the feature disappears. A feature sunset is not an internal deployment event — it is a change that may break workflows customers depend on. The communication and migration plan converts that change from a surprise into a predictable transition: users who follow the migration guide experience no service interruption; users who ignore all notices have received adequate warning. Designing the plan requires knowing who is affected, what the migration path is, and how much time is realistic for each affected cohort to complete the migration.

attrs.alt: Feature sunset communication timeline showing four milestones: T-90 (internal decision), T-60 (public deprecation notice), T-30 (final reminder), T-7 (last-chance warning), and T=0 (feature removed)

content.text: Identifying Affected Users and Assessing Migration Complexity

The first step in any feature sunset plan is building an accurate picture of who uses the feature and how. Sending a generic communication to all users is inefficient and may alarm users who are not affected; sending only to known heavy users risks missing long-tail users who interact with the feature infrequently but would be broken by its removal. The affected user identification uses product telemetry, API usage logs, and support ticket history to build a segmented picture.

The tier method determines the communication channel: Tier 1 consumers (high volume, direct API integrators) require a personal outreach call or direct email from the account team, not just a changelog entry. Tier 2 consumers receive a targeted email with the migration guide and a response deadline. Tier 3 consumers are covered by the public changelog. The max_notice_days_required method sets the minimum sunset window: if any affected consumer has HIGH migration complexity, the window must be at least 90 days, regardless of how simple the migration is for other consumers.
Authoring the Migration Guide

The migration guide is the central artefact of the communication plan. It must answer three questions for every affected consumer: what is being removed, what they should use instead, and exactly what they need to change in their integration. A guide that answers only the first question ("we are removing feature X") without the second and third forces consumers to discover the migration path through trial and error. The guide is published at T-60 and linked from every communication channel.
What is being removed:

State the feature name, the API endpoint or parameter being deprecated, and the exact date of removal. Include the version or release where the feature first appeared so consumers can locate it in their own code.
Why it is being removed:

A brief, honest explanation builds trust. "We are removing this to simplify the API surface" or "this capability is now handled automatically by the model" is more useful than a generic statement. Consumers who understand the reason are better positioned to evaluate whether the replacement actually meets their use case.
The replacement:

Name the replacement endpoint, parameter, or approach. If the replacement is in a different product tier or has different pricing, state that explicitly — a migration that changes the cost profile is not transparent unless the guide says so.
Code examples — before and after:

Show the old API call and the new API call side by side. Use the same programming language as the majority of affected consumers. A migration guide without code examples requires consumers to infer the change from a description — inference introduces errors.
Edge cases and known behaviour differences:

Document any cases where the replacement does not produce identical behaviour to the deprecated feature. A consumer who migrates and discovers an undocumented behaviour difference in production will open a support ticket and lose trust in the process. Known differences disclosed upfront can be planned for.
Support contact:

Include a specific email address or support channel where consumers can ask questions about the migration. Generic "contact support" links discourage engagement; a named point of contact for migration questions increases the acknowledgement rate.
Multi-Channel Communication Sequencing

A single announcement at T-60 is not sufficient. Consumers miss emails, skip changelogs, and let tickets sit in a backlog. The communication plan uses multiple channels with decreasing intervals as the sunset date approaches, calibrated so that a consumer who missed the first three communications still has time to migrate after receiving the fourth.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta

@dataclass
class CommunicationEvent:
    days_before_eol: int
    channel: str                    # "email" | "in_product_banner" | "api_header" | "changelog"
    target_tier: str | None         # None = all tiers
    message_template: str

COMMUNICATION_SCHEDULE = [
    CommunicationEvent(
        days_before_eol=90,
        channel="changelog",
        target_tier=None,
        message_template="Deprecation decision recorded. Migration guide in progress.",
    ),
    CommunicationEvent(
        days_before_eol=60,
        channel="email",
        target_tier="tier1",
        message_template="Direct outreach: {feature} will be removed on {eol_date}. Migration guide attached.",
    ),
    CommunicationEvent(
        days_before_eol=60,
        channel="email",
        target_tier="tier2",
        message_template="Deprecation notice: {feature} is deprecated. See migration guide at {guide_url}.",
    ),
    CommunicationEvent(
        days_before_eol=60,
        channel="in_product_banner",
        target_tier=None,
        message_template="{feature} will be removed on {eol_date}. {guide_url}",
    ),
    CommunicationEvent(
        days_before_eol=30,
        channel="email",
        target_tier="tier1",
        message_template="30-day reminder: {feature} EOL {eol_date}. Have you migrated?",
    ),
    CommunicationEvent(
        days_before_eol=7,
        channel="email",
        target_tier="tier1",
        message_template="URGENT: {feature} removed in 7 days. Unmigrated integrations will break.",
    ),
    CommunicationEvent(
        days_before_eol=7,
        channel="api_header",
        target_tier=None,
        message_template="Sunset-Warning: {feature} removed {eol_date}; migrate to {replacement}",
    ),
]

def events_for_window(eol_date: date, today: date) -> list[CommunicationEvent]:
    days_remaining = (eol_date - today).days
    return [e for e in COMMUNICATION_SCHEDULE if e.days_before_eol <= days_remaining]

The api_header channel at T-7 is the highest-signal communication for API consumers: it appears on every API response from the deprecated endpoint, making it impossible to miss for any consumer actively using the feature in the week before removal. The Sunset-Warning header format (following the pattern Anthropic uses for deprecated model IDs) gives consumers a machine-readable signal that can be monitored in their own infrastructure dashboards, not just noticed by a developer reading response headers manually.
Tracking Acknowledgement and Escalation

Sending a communication is not the same as a consumer being ready for the sunset. The communication plan must include a mechanism to track which consumers have acknowledged the notice and confirmed they are migrated or have a migration plan. For Tier 1 consumers, acknowledgement tracking is manual: the account team confirms the consumer has received and responded to the direct outreach. For Tier 2 and Tier 3 consumers, acknowledgement can be proxied by API telemetry: if a consumer stops using the deprecated feature before the EOL date, they have effectively acknowledged and migrated regardless of whether they responded to the email.

The is_migrated proxy — no deprecated API calls in the last 7 days — is a practical measure that avoids requiring explicit responses from every consumer. Consumers who stop using the deprecated feature are implicitly migrated; the grace_days window accounts for infrequent usage patterns (a weekly batch job that last ran 6 days ago is not necessarily migrated). The escalation_required condition fires for consumers who are still actively calling the deprecated endpoint with fewer than 14 days remaining and have not even acknowledged the email — these consumers need a direct phone call, not another automated email.
Framing: Sunset vs. Replacement vs. Removal

The language used in the communication shapes how consumers respond. "We are removing feature X" triggers alarm. "Feature X is transitioning to the more capable feature Y, which handles your use case automatically" positions the same change as an improvement. The framing is not marketing spin — it is accurate when the replacement genuinely serves the use case better. When the replacement is objectively worse for some use cases (lower availability, different pricing, reduced functionality), the communication must be honest about the trade-off rather than obscuring it with positive framing.
Situation
Recommended Framing
What to Avoid
Better replacement exists
"Transitioning to X which provides Y capability"
Hiding that migration is required
No direct replacement
"Feature X is being removed; closest alternative is Y"
Claiming Y is a replacement when it is not
Higher cost replacement
Explicitly state the pricing difference in the guide
Burying cost changes in footnotes
Removal due to safety concern
State the reason plainly; provide an expedited window if needed
Vague "policy" language that does not help consumers plan

Honest framing builds long-term trust. A consumer who migrates based on an accurate description of the trade-offs and finds exactly what was described will trust the next deprecation notice. A consumer who discovers after migrating that the replacement has limitations that were not disclosed will be adversarial in future deprecation conversations. The communication plan should be reviewed by a technical writer and an affected consumer representative (ideally a Tier 1 account) before publication at T-60.
Internal Readiness Before Publishing the Deprecation Notice

The external deprecation notice must not be published until the internal teams that receive migration questions are ready to answer them. Publishing a deprecation notice when the support team has not been briefed, when the documentation does not yet include the migration guide, and when the account team does not know which of their accounts are affected creates a communication gap: consumers get the notice, attempt to take action, encounter unhelpful support responses and missing documentation, and lose confidence in the migration process.

The rollback_plan_documented check is often overlooked. Even after a deprecation notice is published, it may be necessary to delay the sunset if a critical consumer discovers an unblockable migration issue close to the EOL date. Having a documented rollback plan ("we can extend the EOL date by 30 days, but this requires X engineering work") before the notice is published means the decision to extend can be made quickly if needed, rather than being improvised under pressure with angry consumers waiting.
Handling Consumers Who Do Not Migrate by EOL

Despite thorough communication and adequate notice, a small percentage of consumers will not have completed migration by the EOL date. Some will have missed all communication channels. Some will have planned to migrate but deprioritised it. A few will have an active technical blocker. The handling policy for these consumers must be decided before the sunset date — ideally documented in the original sunset plan — because the decision is made under time pressure and with incomplete information if left to the last week.
Hard cutoff (recommended for planned sunsets):

The deprecated feature is removed on the EOL date as announced. Consumers who have not migrated experience service interruption. This option upholds the credibility of future sunset notices — if extensions are routinely granted, consumers learn to ignore the initial notice.
Emergency extension for documented blockers:

Consumers with a documented technical blocker (reported to support before T-14) can receive an individual extension of 14-30 days while the blocker is resolved. The extension requires a signed commitment date and active engineering engagement. No extension is granted without documentation — a consumer who did not report a blocker has no basis for an emergency extension.
Graceful degradation period (alternative for high-impact removals):

For features with a large number of affected consumers, a 14-day graceful degradation period after the EOL date can reduce the blast radius: the deprecated feature returns a warning response rather than an error, giving consumers who missed the deadline a final chance to migrate before hard removal. This option increases the risk that some consumers treat the warning as acceptable and never migrate.

The policy must be documented in the public deprecation notice. Consumers who know that extensions are available only for documented technical blockers are motivated to report blockers early and to complete migrations on time to avoid the bureaucratic overhead of requesting an extension. Ambiguity about the extension policy — leaving it unstated — incentivises consumers to assume an extension will be available and reduces migration urgency.
Documentation Lifecycle and Post-Sunset Record

After the EOL date, the deprecated feature's documentation must be updated to reflect its removal. Leaving old documentation accessible without marking it as removed creates confusion: consumers searching for the feature find the original documentation, attempt to use it, and only discover the removal when API calls fail. The post-sunset documentation policy must address three artefacts: the original API reference entry, the migration guide, and the changelog.

The should_keep_migration_guide method encodes a deliberate retention policy: migration guides do not disappear on the EOL date. Consumers who encounter legacy code that references the deprecated feature 6 months after its removal still need the migration guide to understand what changed and how to update the code. A migration guide that 404s one day after the feature is removed is useless for this purpose. Two years is a conservative retention period that covers the typical time horizon of code reviews and legacy codebases; it can be extended for features with large enterprise consumer bases that operate on longer upgrade cycles.
Sunset Plan Template and Checklist

Every feature sunset should be planned using a consistent template. A template prevents teams from designing the process from scratch each time, ensures that steps are not forgotten under time pressure, and creates a standard format that can be reviewed by the programme manager and legal/compliance functions if needed. The template covers the six phases of the sunset plan.
Phase
Owner
Key Deliverable
Timing
Scope and consumer identification
Product manager
AffectedConsumer list with tiers and complexity
T-90 to T-75
Migration guide authoring
Technical writer + engineer
Published, reviewed guide at guide_url
T-75 to T-60
Internal readiness
Programme manager
InternalReadinessChecklist fully green
T-62 to T-60
External communication
Account team + comms
All COMMUNICATION_SCHEDULE events sent on time
T-60 through T-7
EOL execution
Engineering on-call
Feature removed; incidents resolved within MTTR target
T=0 to T+24h
Post-sunset cleanup
Technical writer + PM
Docs updated; SunsetDocumentationRecord filed; retrospective scheduled
T+1d to T+7d

The six-phase template assigns explicit ownership to each phase. Without ownership, the migration guide can be deprioritised by the engineering team until T-65 (leaving only 5 days for review before publication), the internal readiness check can be skipped under time pressure, and the post-sunset cleanup can be forgotten entirely. The template converts the sunset plan from an informally understood process to a tracked deliverable with named owners and clear timing.
Summary

Feature sunset communication is a programme management discipline that runs in parallel with the technical work of removing the feature. The effective plan segments affected consumers by volume and migration complexity, publishes a concrete migration guide at T-60, uses multiple communication channels with increasing urgency as the sunset date approaches, and tracks acknowledgement through both direct responses and API telemetry. Consumers receive enough advance notice to complete even high-complexity migrations before the deadline, and escalation processes exist for the small cohort that remains unacknowledged or unmigrated in the final two weeks. A well-executed sunset leaves no consumer surprised and no integrations broken on the day the feature is removed.
